scenes.vo: March fifteenth, 2026. Sunday morning, seven forty-five Eastern Time. Across three continents, pharmaceutical production systems begin to disappear. StonaWave has six hours to understand what PharmLeaks has already spent weeks preparing. This is Operation Bitter Pill.
scenes.title: BITTER PILL
scenes.subtitle: The StonaWave ransomware crisis

scenes.vo: The visible crisis starts on Sunday. The real attack started three weeks earlier, with one valid contractor V P N credential. PharmLeaks moves through Active Directory, maps the backup infrastructure, exfiltrates clinical data, and stages ransomware through trusted deployment tools. At seven forty-five, everything detonates at once.
scenes.heading: Twenty-one days of advantage.

nodes.title: Valid access purchased
nodes.sub: A contractor VPN credential opens the first door

nodes.title: Lateral movement
nodes.sub: Excessive trust exposes corporate and industrial identity

nodes.title: Backups mapped
nodes.sub: Catalogs, replication jobs and recovery paths identified

nodes.title: Data exfiltrated
nodes.sub: Patient records, clinical trials and regulatory files

scenes.vo: The blast crosses StonaWave's global operating model. Manufacturing execution systems stop in New Jersey, Frankfurt, and Hyderabad. The clinical trial platform, E R P, and identity services fail. At headquarters near Paris, the crisis team cannot yet tell whether medicines, research, or patient data are safe.
scenes.heading: One identity plane. Four sites.
scenes.statLabel: sites in crisis across three continents

scenes.vo: By midday, this is no longer only a recovery problem. PharmLeaks demands twenty-five million dollars and claims to hold two point four terabytes of patient and clinical-trial data. Critical oncology supply is at risk. The company faces operational disruption, extortion, and a data breach at the same time.
scenes.heading: Three crises. One clock.

facts.sub: for a decryptor and a promise of deletion
patient records, clinical trials and regulatory submissions
quality release and partner supply flows are blocked
while the attacker controls the public narrative

scenes.vo: The crisis escapes the network. A manufacturing partner reports disruption. CERT France and international regulators request immediate facts. News coverage appears in French, English, and German. Then PharmLeaks threatens to publish stolen patient data. Every new audience creates a new deadline.
scenes.eyebrow: Act II — H6 · The crisis goes public
scenes.heading: Every border adds pressure

stamps.name: REGULATORS
stamps.fig: patient and clinical data at risk

bottom.strong: The leak threat drives the crisis.

scenes.vo: StonaWave refuses payment after Hyderabad isolates early and investigators find a clean immutable backup. Recovery moves into a clean room: identity first, then E R P, clinical systems, and manufacturing. PharmLeaks publishes a first data sample on day two. Critical oncology production resumes in stages by day five.
scenes.heading: Rebuild trust before systems
scenes.sub: critical oncology production resumes in stages
scenes.strip:
Immutable backup · clean identities
controlled restoration · monitored production
scenes.footer: Recovery is a sequence of trusted decisions.

scenes.vo: Three lessons for the board. A stolen credential is only the entry point; excessive shared trust creates the blast radius. Backups are a governance system, not just storage. And a global recovery plan only exists if every site has tested it. Resilience begins before the crisis.
scenes.eyebrow: What this means for the board

lessons.text: Control partner access and reduce shared trust.
Protect and govern recovery infrastructure as a critical service.
Test global recovery with every business and manufacturing site.
